item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea, but I feel like there's not enough information on what the crack entity is. I mean, I know that's the point, but I think it would have been more interesting if we actually had some kind of hint. For all we know, it's just some monster that eats people and talks in vaguely mysterious phrases.

I'm probably missing something here, aren't I?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really, really enjoy most of it, although… eh that audio log pushed it too far for me. I like a bit more of my mystery left properly mysterious to mull over, so I think I'm in the minority but I'm of the opinion it'd be stronger without it.

Still pretty good though. Very solid and enjoyable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really like this one.

Small error I noticed. The first D-class employee form lists them as being employed in 2019 but the notes under that list it as starting in 2018.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Haven't read it yet, but in the first attachment it says ''see see'' instead of see

EDIT: that was a really fun read, thanks for writing it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't necessarily disagree substantively with your assessment, but do want to say the author explicitly stated in page 1 of the comments that the footnotes were set up like that for a reason namely, they said it would have broken the formatting, which makes sense at least to me.
Yay

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The footnotes don't work, the jargon/writing is filled with words that sacrifice readability for "intelligent" tone, some portions were uninteresting enough to skip, notably the foundation employment records, there's a portion of the first bit of the article that's basically "here's a long list of names we'll add and never bring up again" and this, in general, feels like a rough draft of something that could be good if someone took an axe to it. The "metal building spooky light foundation anomaly" thing feels… like it's trying too hard and not hard enough at the same time. Like there is potential for the concept, but with a rewrite.

Decent potential for a regular skip and/or -01 proposal (?) with a heavy rewrite, but currently unreadable and just not a very enjoyable experience with the layout and format. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you recall, the first D-class subject had actually been exposed to 5000 twice - once without the epsilon value and once with the epsilon value, so his net value (during the second test) would be 2.000 counting the Ishvara event.

The second is a legitimate mistake, whoops!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, this was a lot of fun. This sort of thing has been dome before, but it's a lovely, original sort of twist on it, and I enjoy the narrative progression.

Nitpicks: the first D-class test subject's epsilon value is given as 2.000, when I presume it should have been 1.000. The dates of the first D-class employment history given are in reverse order.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can you please elaborate?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The dates listed are like that intentionally, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's a perfectly reasonable gripe, although it is kind of the main point of the article. Loss of free will is one of my greatest fears, and I wanted to explore it here with the theme of mystery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, it was that the SCPF members are sort of predestined to be SCPF members as far as this piece is concerned. To me, that removes a huge chunk of the free will that makes the SCPF staff so interesting, that they choose to live this life in the shadows to some capacity.

Additionally, I find the Administrator here to be more of a McGuffin than anything else. moving your plot forward without any real agency apparent.

Perhaps I am wrong in my interpretation though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If I may ask, what specific aspect of the ending ruined the mystery for you? I tried to make it relatively easy to digest, but I also don't want it to be so obvious that it elicits no excitement.

I admit I distanced my portrayal of the Administrator from other authors' iterations, since he isn't a very important part of the piece. Then again, most works feature him far more heavily than mine, so perhaps it might come as an anticlimax.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The technobabble about antimemes in the footnotes isn't exactly required reading; I included it mostly for the verisimilitude. I can definitely see how the intimidating sciencey stuff in the rest of the piece might muddle some readers' understanding, though.

I'm admittedly not a big fan of the tabview look myself, so I'm probably going to change the formatting to something akin to that of Shaggy's old entry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The "footnotes" are just colored numbers in superscript made to mimic their look, since actual footnotes spawn at the very bottom of the page and would have broke the formatting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmmmm.

The opening hook was pretty good and did set the stage well for the mystery you were trying to tell. That said, when we get to the big reveal at the end, wherein we learn about the building's connection to the administrator, and the reasoning behind the now need to assign the Foundation itself as an anomaly, the whole mystery just kind of unravels and fizzles out for me as a reader.

Additionally, for all the portrayals of the Administrator we have on the site, this one was just kind of meh.

No vote. I can understand why others like this article, and there are some bits I enjoy myself, but all in all it doesn't do much for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found this impenetrable thanks to the jargon, especially in regards to the antimemes. The definitions themselves make no sense to me, and all I took away from this was "spooky building, spooky crack, light and noise and the Foundation is an anomaly somehow". The "only read below if you want The Truth" opener did not help things any.

On a different tack, I feel the formatting was a bad choice, too. Had this been a series of collapsibles instead of a tabview, it might look better, or at least I wouldn't have had to keep scrolling back to the top to find the next tab. I suppose you want the verisimilitude of being able to scroll to the bottom and get the amnestic at any time, but I think it'd be best if you considered putting the reader into the pants of someone who had decided to read the whole thing. As it stands, -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno why,but the footnote code doesn't work.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: Oh, I've never heard of him. If anything, I figured it had something to do with Zyn if it wasn't fully original, lol.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded:
Thank you!

The prose at the end is actually a quote from the Chinese philosopher Zhuangzi - I don't want to take the credit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was sort of my intention; I wanted that "glitchy" feel in the last addendum. I could see how it might be a bit confusing to some, though.
This is still super great! I like the extended prose at the end, too, good work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I also see those. I think they are intentional, according to https://www.babelstone.co.uk/Unicode/whatisit.html they're all the same character. @tupacofficial I think you should consider replacing those, because � is usually seen when there is a missing font problem. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Specials_(Unicode_block)#Replacement_character

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked this; it's mysterious, but not mysterious to the point I have zero clue what's going on.
Also, a question: are the �s at the very end supposed to be �s, or is this a display problem?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article has one of the best hooks for any piece I've read.

Would you like to go down the rabbit hole? Yes please.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great stuff. This has a similar feel to Clef's 001, but better evokes that sense of mystery and unease.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I quite like it. It's short and sweet, and while the "omg the foundation is the scp" angle has been explored a lot, I feel like this piece ties it into the mystery angle, and leaves you with a lot of questions regarding why the people in the employ of the foundation do what they do. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
game over losers

Thanks to Uncannyon A Random Day TrustyOlValet2 Cyvstvi13 Cerastes for looking over this winning piece

Preferred numbers:

5055
5099
5200
5678
5990

Screw it, just give me the earliest available slot.
